title: Safe handling and moving livestock
subtitle: How to move sheep, goats, and pigs calmly and safely without causing stress or injury.
excerpt: Livestock that have been handled calmly and consistently are easier to work with than those that have only been approached in a panic. The principles are the same across species: understand the animal's flight zone, move slowly, use the animal's natural tendency to follow others.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep. Temple Grandin, Livestock Handling and Transport (4th edition). AHDB, livestock handling and stress reduction guide.

content.text: Calm stock management starts before you enter the field. Livestock that can hear or smell you before you appear are less startled than those surprised at close range. Walk quietly, avoid sudden movements, and give the animals time to register your presence at the edge of their
To move an animal forward, enter the flight zone from slightly behind the

at the shoulder. To stop or slow the animal, move forward past the point of balance. Livestock instinctively move away from pressure applied within the flight zone; the key is applying that pressure in the right direction. Do not chase; pressure and release is more effective than continuous pressure.

The lead animal will determine whether the rest follow. Hold the gate partially open and position yourself so the first animal can see a clear, unobstructed route through. Do not stand directly in the gateway; stand to the side so the animal can pass without having to move toward you. Once the first animal goes through, the rest will usually follow naturally. Give them time; rushing the gate causes the lead animal to stop and the group to mill and scatter.

A shepherd's crook extends your effective reach to catch a sheep's neck or a back leg without chasing the animal. Loop the neck smoothly as the animal passes and apply firm but even pressure; a jerk will pull the animal over. Once held, bring your other hand down the body to secure the animal against you. Never use the crook to strike or prod an animal.

Livestock that are accustomed to human presence and regular low-stress handling are significantly easier to work with in an emergency. Spend time near the animals without always doing something to them. Call before you approach, especially when supplementary feeding; animals that associate your presence with food will come to you rather than run from you.
